sanskrit_devanagari:
गुरूनहत्वा हि महानुभावान्
श्रेयो भोक्तुं भैक्ष्यमपीह लोके ।
हत्वार्थकामांस्तु गुरूनिहैव
भुञ्जीय भोगान्रुधिरप्रदिग्धान् ॥ ५ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
gurūn ahatvā hi mahānubhāvān
śreyo bhoktuṁ bhaikṣyam apīha loke |
hatvārtha-kāmāṁs tu gurūn ihaiva
bhuñjīya bhogān rudhira-pradigdhān || 5 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical)
exact (text body identical; trishtubh meter — 4 quarters of 11 syllables)
exact (academic critical edition; Bhg_02.005 = MBh_06,024.005). Body text identical.

translations.verbatim_quote: It would be better to live in this world by begging than to live at the cost of the lives of great souls who are my teachers. Even though desiring worldly gain, they are superiors. If they are killed, everything we enjoy will be tainted with blood.

It would be better to live in this world by begging, than to enjoy life by killing these noble elders, who are my teachers. If we kill them, the wealth and pleasures we enjoy will be tainted with blood.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: According to scriptural codes, a teacher who engages in an abominable action and has lost his sense of discrimination is fit to be abandoned. Bhīṣma and Droṇa were obliged to take the side of Duryodhana because of his financial assistance, although they should not have accepted such a position simply on financial considerations.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.copyright_holder: Advaita Ashrama, Kolkata (publisher of Swami Gambhirananda's English translation of Sankara-bhasya). No verbatim is captured because no commentary exists for this verse.
commentaries.notes: Site bhagavad-gita.us states explicitly that Sri Sankaracharya did not comment on this sloka and that his commentary starts from 2.10. Standard Advaita Ashrama (Gambhirananda) edition agrees - Sankara's bhasya begins at BG 2.10.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads this verse as Arjuna offering renunciation as a substitute for action — proposing the bhikṣu (mendicant) life as preferable to the kṣatriya's duty. The chapter will reject this; renunciation in this case is wrong-renunciation because it is grounded in aversion (the blood of teachers) rather than in steadiness of knowledge.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (per Sri Ramakrishna Math edition; bhagavad-gita.us reproduces this English rendering of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.4 – 2.5 Arjuna said — Again Arjuna, being moved by love, compassion and fear, mistaking unrighteousness for righteousness, and not understanding, i.e., not knowing the beneficial words of Sri Krsna, said as follows: ‘How can I slay Bhisma, Drona and others worthy or reverence?
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja notes the qualifier 'artha-kāmān' (those who themselves desire wealth) — Arjuna concedes that the elders are not pure in motive on this battlefield. But he argues that even tainted teachers are still teachers, and the blood-stained kingdom would be unenjoyable. The argument is psychologically real and dharmically incoherent simultaneously.

positions.rendering: the enjoyments themselves bear the stain — sense-aversion, not moral judgment
psychologically unenjoyable, not necessarily ritually impure

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All translators render the phrase identically; the commentarial disagreement is whether 'tainted with blood' refers to the metaphysical impurity of the enjoyments or to Arjuna's projected inability to take pleasure in them. The distinction matters because the chapter will treat Arjuna's projected unenjoyability as a feeling-state, not a binding fact about the world.

literal_meaning: Indeed, not having killed the great-souled teachers, it is better to live by begging in this world. But having killed those elders (even though they desire wealth), I would enjoy here only blood-smeared enjoyments.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Arjuna's second argument: even if the action is technically permitted, the consequences will be unbearable. Better the bhikṣu's begging-bowl than a kingdom soaked in the blood of teachers. The verse is doctrinally important because it introduces the false-equivalence Arjuna is making: he is comparing the imagined post-victory state ('blood-smeared enjoyments') with an idealised alternative ('begging in this world'), and the imagined comparison feels asymmetric. The chapter will diagnose this as projection — not as accurate forecasting.

remaining_gaps: Shankara verbatim — site states he did not comment on 2.5; bhasya begins at 2.10.
